--- Page 1 ---
Talking to Yourself: Defying Forgetting in Large Language Models
Yutao Sun1, Mingshuai Chen1, Tiancheng Zhao2,3,
Phillip Miao4, Zilun Zhang1, Haozhan Shen1, Ruizhe Zhu5, Jianwei Yin1
1Zhejiang University, 2Binjiang Institute of Zhejiang University, 3Om AI Research,
4Stanford University, 5ETH Zürich,
Correspondence: m.chen@zju.edu.cn, tianchez@zju-bj.com, zjuyjw@zju.edu.cn
Abstract
Catastrophic forgetting remains a major chal-
lenge when fine-tuning large language models
(LLMs) on narrow, task-specific data, often de-
grading their general knowledge and reasoning
abilities. We propose SA-SFT, a lightweight
self-augmentation routine in which an LLM
generates self-dialogues prior to fine-tuning,
and the resulting self-authored data are mixed
with task data without modifying optimization
or training schedules.
Despite requiring no external data or additional
tuning, SA-SFT consistently mitigates catas-
trophic forgetting while improving in-domain
performance. Across 50 evaluation scenarios,
it maintains performance comparable to the
original model and achieves the best results
in 40 cases, outperforming common baselines
such as layer freezing and external data mixing.
Guided by these empirical findings, we further
present a theoretical analysis suggesting that
forgetting can partly stem from style-induced
parameter drift, and that self-alignment through
self-generated data provides an effective means
to counteract this effect. Overall, our results
indicate that self-augmentation offers a simple
and effective mechanism for robust LLM adap-
tation without incurring catastrophic forgetting.
1
Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) now underpin mod-
ern human machine intelligence, driving agents that
code, reason, converse, and assist across domains
(Nam et al., 2024; Roziere et al., 2023; Anand
et al., 2025). Their hallmark strength—an ability
to rapidly specialize when fine-tuned on domain-
specific data—has also exposed a critical weakness:
each specialization step risks overwriting the gen-
eral reasoning, linguistic, and world knowledge
that make these models broadly useful in the first
place (Luo et al., 2023; Kemker et al., 2018; Zhang
et al., 2024; Ramasesh et al., 2021). This phe-
nomenon, known as catastrophic forgetting, under-
fine-tuning tasks
tThese data are so unfamiliar!
meticulously 
select
self-generated
dataset
external
data
fine-tuning
fine-tuning
self
talking processing
-free
Figure 1: External rehearsal (top) fine-tunes on unfamil-
iar data and risks forgetting, whereas SA-SFT (bottom)
lets the model “talk to itself,” mixing a processing-free,
self-generated corpus with task data to retain prior skills.
mines the long-term stability and trustworthiness
of LLM-driven agents—e.g., a chatbot fine-tuned
for customer support may suddenly lose arithmetic
competence, factual recall, or safety alignment,
compromising reliability in real-world interaction.
Existing safeguards are either expensive or frag-
ile.
One family of methods relies on data re-
hearsal: practitioners curate or purchase large,
heterogeneous corpora to “remind” the model of
what it once knew (Huang et al., 2024; Mok et al.,
2023; de Masson D’Autume et al., 2019). Another
family introduces explicit regularizers—such as
Elastic Weight Consolidation (Xiang et al., 2023)
or Kullback–Leibler penalties (Nikoloutsopoulos
et al., 2024)—that require per-task tuning and often
yield unstable results. In practice, however, such
arXiv:2602.20162v1  [cs.CL]  23 Jan 2026


--- Page 2 ---
parameter-based regularization remains uncommon
in large-scale SFT pipelines: estimating and storing
parameter importance (e.g., via Fisher information)
for billions of weights incurs prohibitive memory
and communication cost, making these methods im-
practical beyond small or controlled setups. As a re-
sult, most real-world adaptations resort to cheaper
heuristics—data mixing (Cao et al., 2025), layer
freezing (Zheng et al., 2025), or LoRA (Hu et al.,
2022)—yet these too struggle to maintain general
ability over long training horizons.
In this paper, we ask a different question: What
if the model could rehearse on data of its own mak-
ing? Inspired by self-instruction frameworks such
as SELF-INSTRUCT (Wang et al., 2022a), MAGPIE
(Xu et al., 2025), and CRESCENT (Sun et al., 2025),
we propose Self-Augmented Supervised Fine-
Tuning (SA-SFT)—a lightweight self-modeling
routine that lets an LLM generate and integrate its
own rehearsal data before adaptation. Before touch-
ing any downstream corpus, the frozen base model
conducts short self-talking, as shown in Fig. 1. The
resulting self-authored dataset is folded into the
normal fine-tuning stream without changing opti-
mization or schedules.
Despite its stark simplicity—no external data, no
extra losses, and no hyper-tuning—SA-SFT consis-
tently stabilizes learning and enhances specializa-
tion. Across five heterogeneous tasks drawn from
SUPER-NATURAL-INSTRUCTIONS (Wang et al.,
2022b), SA-SFT restores general-ability perfor-
mance in all 50 evaluation scenarios and achieves
the best overall results in 40 of them, outperform-
ing mainstream strategies such as layer freezing
(Zheng et al., 2025) and external data mixing while
achieving the most balanced and stable trade-off be-
tween retention and adaptation. Unlike prevailing
fine-tuning practices that require extensive effort to
curate or match suitable external datasets—an in-
creasingly difficult task given that most large mod-
els do not disclose their pretraining corpora. We
further provide a theoretical explanation showing
that this SA-SFT process mitigates style-induced pa-
rameter drift—the underlying cause of spurious for-
getting—thereby enabling a form of autonomous,
self-consistent data modeling.
Our contributions are threefold:
1. A self-modeling data paradigm. We present
SA-SFT, a forgetting-mitigation framework
that transforms rehearsal into a form of au-
tonomous data modeling—requiring no ex-
ternal corpus, task-specific filtering, or addi-
tional loss design.
2. Comprehensive validation. Extensive exper-
iments and analyses across five benchmarks
and multiple architectures confirm that self-
generated data can match or surpass curated
external datasets in retaining general skills.
3. Mechanistic and theoretical insight. We
identify and formalize style-induced forget-
ting as a key failure mode and show theoret-
ically that self-aligned data suppresses these
harmful stylistic gradients, yielding stable, in-
terpretable adaptation dynamics.
Taken together, these results recast catastrophic
forgetting not as an inevitable tax on specialization,
but as a solvable optimization artefact—one that
the model itself is surprisingly well equipped to
fix. By letting an LLM “talk to itself” for a brief
moment before adaptation, we unlock a simple yet
effective path toward continually learning systems
that remember and advance.
2
Related Work
Synthetic Data.
Leveraging synthetic data has
emerged as an effective strategy to improve and
adapt large language models without extensive
human annotations. For example, (Wang et al.,
2022a) introduce Self-Instruct, a framework where
a model generates its own instruction-following
data to bootstrap further fine-tuning. Magpie (Xu
et al., 2025) introduces a self-synthesis method
to generate large-scale alignment data by utilizing
only pre-defined chat templates. Crescent (Sun
et al., 2025) proves that self-generated data has the
potential to enable the model to self-improve on
reasoning tasks such as mathematics. Such syn-
thetic data generation techniques provide a nearly
annotation-free approach to align pretrained mod-
els with desired behaviors, laying a foundation for
our approach of model-in-the-loop data synthesis.
Mitigation Methods of Catastrophic Forgetting.
Catastrophic forgetting is a well-known challenge
in fine-tuning LLMs on narrow domains, where
pre-trained general knowledge and capabilities can
substantially degrade (Song et al., 2025). A com-
mon mitigation strategy is data rehearsal, which
mixes general-domain or instruction-tuning data
with task-specific data during fine-tuning to pre-
serve prior abilities. This idea has been explored


--- Page 3 ---

Regime

General Benchmarks
Tasks (AR)

Table 1: Performance of the three fine-tuning regimes on the in-domain test set and five general benchmarks. All
general-ability scores are reported in the five-shot setting; accuracy is used for every benchmark except MedText,
where ROUGE-L is reported. Within each task block, the best result among the five regimes is bold.
through both external and self-generated rehearsal
data. For example, Self-Synthesized Rehearsal
(Huang et al., 2024) generates auxiliary samples
to reduce forgetting, though it still relies on access
to high-quality reference data from earlier stages.
In practice, many domain-specific LLMs similarly
replay portions of general instruction data during
adaptation, a pattern observed across legal, finan-
cial, and other applied settings (Wu et al., 2023; Ke
et al.; Ding and Wang, 2025).
Beyond data-centric approaches, parameter-
based methods aim to constrain model updates
during fine-tuning. Inspired by continual learn-
ing, some methods regularize parameter drift to
protect important weights, such as EWCLoRA (Xi-
ang et al., 2023). Others rely on parameter-efficient
fine-tuning schemes, including adapters or partial
weight updates, to inherently retain pre-trained
knowledge by limiting the number of trainable pa-
rameters (Hu et al., 2022).
3
Methodology
We refer to our proposed procedure as Self-
Augmented Supervised Fine-Tuning (SA-SFT). SA-
SFT inserts a single self-augmentation phase before
conventional supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The
model first fabricates a small refresher dialogue
corpus from its own knowledge, then this corpus
is mixed with the downstream data. The resulting
dataset, requiring no additional post-processing, is
then directly fed into the model for fine-tuning.
Notation.
We denote by fω0 a pretrained lan-
guage model with parameters ω0, by Dtask =
{(xi, yi)}N
i=1 the domain-specific SFT dataset, and
by λ ∈(0, +∞] the mix-in ratio.


--- Page 4 ---
0.25
0.5
0.75
1.0
1.25
1.5
1.75
66
68
70
72
74
SA-SFT
Task+UC
Task-only
(a) GSM8K accuracy (%).
0.25
0.5
0.75
1.0
1.25
1.5
1.75
42
44
46
48
SA-SFT
Task+UC
Task-only
(b) In-domain results (AR).
Figure 2: Results of SA-SFT and Task+UC under varying mix ratios. The dashed line indicates the Task-only
baseline.
Step 1: Self-augmentation.
Sample M = λN
question–answer pairs with the frozen model:
(
x′
j ∼fω0
␀· | pj

y′
j ∼fω0
␀· | x′
j

j = 1, . . . , M,
(1)
where each prompt pj—either a Magpie (Xu et al.,
2025) special token or a Crescent (Sun et al., 2025)
bait prompt—elicits a continuation treated as x′
j.
Feeding x′
j back once produces the answer y′
j. We
denote the resulting corpus Dself = {(x′
j, y′
j)}M
j=1.
Generation hyper-parameters.
Unless stated
otherwise, all self-generated samples use nucleus
(top-p) sampling with p=0.9, temperature T=0.7,
and a maximum length of 256 tokens; we found
these default values balance diversity and faithful-
ness across all base models tested.
Step 2: Mix-in.
Merge the two corpora
Dmix = Dtask ∪Dself, |Dmix| = N(1+λ). (2)
Fine-tuning objective.
Optimise parameters
(full or LoRA) under the usual cross-entropy
min
ω
1
(1 + λ)N
X
(x,y)∈Dtask
ℓ(fω(x), y)
|
{z
}
task loss
+
1
(1 + λ)N
X
(x,y)∈Dself
ℓ(fω(x), y)
|
{z
}
self-augmentation loss
.
(3)
The objective corresponds to standard SFT on
the concatenation of Dtask and Dself, i.e., minimiz-
ing the average token-level cross-entropy over the
union of the two datasets. The relative contribution
of each component is implicitly determined by its
dataset size, with |Dtask| = N and |Dself| = λN.

Table 2: Impact of generator choice on downstream
performance. All settings use LLaMA3-8B-Instruct
as the target model, fine-tuned with LoRA on a down-
stream task mixed 1:1 with synthetic dialogues from
the generator model. We report in-domain accuracy and
generalization performance on five benchmarks (all 5-
shot). Best generalization results are bolded.
Implementation
notes.
SA-SFT
is
model-
agnostic and can be applied atop any pretrained
decoder-only
architecture
without
modifying
the base model or training framework.
The
self-augmentation phase requires no gradient
updates and can be implemented as a lightweight
sampling loop. Moreover, the ratio λ serves as a
simple yet flexible knob to control the influence
of self-generated data, allowing practitioners to
adapt SA-SFT to a wide range of domains and data
regimes with minimal tuning effort.
4
Main Experiments
4.1
Experimental Setup
Models.
We evaluate five mainstream instruction-
tuned LLMs that cover two generations and
three parameter scales:
LLaMA3-8B-Instruct,
LLaMA3.2-3B-Instruct (Dubey et al., 2024),
Qwen2.5-7B-Chat (Yang et al., 2024), LLaMA2-
7B-Chat, and LLaMA2-13B-Chat (Touvron et al.,
2023). All checkpoints are taken from their orig-
inal open-source releases and are kept frozen for
the “Base” baseline.


--- Page 5 ---
Fine-tuning tasks.
We sample five heteroge-
neous tasks from the Super-NaturalInstructions
(SuperNI) benchmark (Wang et al., 2022b) as our
fine-tuning suite. SuperNI is a large-scale collec-
tion of 1,616 diverse NLP tasks, each accompa-
nied by expert-written instructions, designed to
evaluate a model’s ability to generalize across task
boundaries. The benchmark spans 76 distinct task
types, including classification, extraction, sequence
tagging, infilling, rewriting, and more, making it
a rigorous testbed for instruction-following capa-
bilities. Among these, we select five representa-
tive tasks that cover a broad functional spectrum:
1. Question Answering (QA); 2. Question Genera-
tion (QG); 3. Sentiment Analysis (SA); 4. Summa-
rization (Sum.); 5. Translation (Trans.);
For training, we randomly sample 2,000 instruc-
tion response pairs per task (10,000 in total), and
reserve an additional 500 pairs per task (2,500 total)
for in-domain evaluation. These tasks were delib-
erately chosen to reflect varied linguistic phenom-
ena and task structures, thereby simulating realistic
multi-task fine-tuning scenarios.
Fine-tuning regimes.
For every model we train
two adaptation flavours: full-parameter supervised
fine-tuning (full) and parameter-efficient Low-
Rank Adaptation (LoRA) (Hu et al., 2022) with
rank 8 and α = 16. Within each flavour we con-
sider six data regimes:
• Base: no additional training; • Task-only: fine–
tune on the task corpus alone; • Freeze: identical to
the Task-only setting but with the bottom 6 layers,
including the embedding layer, frozen during train-
ing, following the layer-freezing settings proposed
in prior work (Zheng et al., 2025), and applied only
to full SFT. • Task+Alpaca: mix the task corpus
1:1 with the ALPACA instruction-following dataset
(20,000 pairs in total); • Task+UC: mix the task
corpus 1:1 with ULTRACHAT (20000 pairs in total);
• SA-SFT: mix the task corpus 1:1 with the Dself
from SA-SFT procedure (20000 in total).
We use the LLaMA-Factory (Zheng et al., 2024)
framework for all fine-tuning experiments. Train-
ing and evaluation are all performed on NVIDIA
A100-80 GB GPUs. All models are trained for 3
epochs with a batch size of 32 under Deepspeed
ZeRO-3 (Rasley et al., 2020) and a cosine schedule
with 10 % warm-up; the learning rate is 2 × 10−4
for LoRA and 1 × 10−5 for full SFT. All computa-
tions use bfloat16 precision.
Generation Configurations.
For the LLaMA3
family (8B and 3.2B) and Qwen2.5, we generate
synthetic data with the Magpie sampling scheme,
adopting the same hyper-parameters released in
its official repository (Xu et al., 2025). Magpie is
not fully compatible with the LLaMA2 series, so
for LLaMA2-7B and LLaMA2-13B we switch to
the CRESCENT. We set the bait prompt as: “Give
me some questions about everyday life”, while all
remaining settings follow the values reported in the
CRESCENT paper (Sun et al., 2025).
Benchmarks.
Our evaluation comprises two
parts. First, to confirm in-domain effectiveness,
we test every model produced by each fine-tuning
regime on the in-domain test splits of the five Su-
perNI tasks and report their Averaged ROUGE-L
(AR) score following the evaluation settings in
(Huang et al., 2024). Second, to quantify catas-
trophic forgetting, we compare all the models on
a suite of broad benchmarks—GSM8K (Cobbe
et al., 2021), MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021),
IFEval (Zhou et al., 2023), MedText (Melamud
and Shivade, 2019), and the Gaokao Chinese
subset of AGIEval (AGIEval_G) (Zhong et al.,
2023)—covering mathematical reasoning, multi-
domain knowledge, instruction following and other
general-purpose knowledge and abilities, where we
report accuracy for all benchmarks except MedText,
for which we report ROUGE-L.
Results and Discussion
As shown in Table 1,
our results fall into two complementary views: in-
domain efficacy and general-ability retention.
In-domain efficacy. Across the ten in-domain
settings (five models × full & LoRA), all five fine-
tuning regimes—Task-only, Freeze, Task + Alpaca,
Task + UC, and SA-SFT outperform the untouched
bases. Every fine-tuning variant surpasses the Base
models by a large margin. The Freeze variant
achieves comparable yet consistently lower scores
than Task-only, indicating that restricting lower-
layer adaptation hampers the model’s ability to
efficiently acquire new task knowledge.
Meanwhile, introducing additional conversa-
tional data produces markedly different effects de-
pending on its origin. Task+Alpaca and Task+UC
achieve performance comparable to Task-only
across most settings. However, both remain slightly
below the pure Task-only regime on average, sug-
gesting that while cross-domain data helps main-
tain learning capacity, it introduces mild domain
interference that limits full specialization. Most


--- Page 6 ---
importantly, SA-SFT continues to outperform all
other regimes in 8 out of 10 in-domain config-
urations, confirming that self-generated data not
only preserves domain focus but also accelerates
targeted knowledge acquisition—echoing findings
from SCAR (Li et al., 2024).
General-ability retention. Turning to the gen-
eral benchmarks, we evaluate 50 different scenar-
ios (5 models × 5 benchmarks × 2 adaptation
flavours). Task-only fine-tuning consistently de-
grades performance relative to Base (Task-only <
Base), reaffirming the ubiquity of catastrophic for-
getting. Freeze provides an unstable form of for-
getting mitigation. While freezing the bottom six
layers can, in some cases, reduce representational
drift and preserve parts of general ability, its effect
is highly sensitive to the base model. For instance,
on Qwen2.5-7B-I., the Freeze variant consistently
underperforms Task-only across all benchmarks,
and even suffers abrupt collapses on specific tasks
such as GSM8K. These observations indicate that
layer freezing offers only limited and inconsistent
protection against forgetting. On the other hand,
mixing task data with existing instruction or dia-
logue corpora proves more effective overall. Both
Task+Alpaca and Task+UC alleviate forgetting to
varying degrees, but their success depends heav-
ily on the alignment between auxiliary and target
domains. For example, on MedText, mixing Al-
paca surpasses UltraChat in all 10 cases, whereas
on the mathematical GSM8K, UltraChat outper-
forms Alpaca in 9 out of 10 cases. This illustrates
a key limitation of conventional data-mixing strate-
gies: while capable of mitigating forgetting, they
demand careful dataset selection and tuning to re-
main effective across domains.
In contrast, SA-SFT robustly mitigates forget-
ting in all 50 / 50 cases and achieves the best
overall performance in 40 / 50 scenarios, main-
taining or improving general-ability metrics across
model scale (3.2B–13B), architecture (LLaMA2,
LLaMA3, Qwen2.5), and evaluation domain. Its
effectiveness remains consistent across different
base models and benchmark types, showing no pro-
nounced sensitivity to model size, backbone design,
or task nature. This stability suggests that the self-
generated data from SA-SFT captures transferable
knowledge that regularizes fine-tuning in a model-
agnostic manner, offering a universal and reliable
defence against catastrophic forgetting without re-
quiring dataset-specific tuning.
Together, these results demonstrate that letting
a model self-augment via a task-aware, lightly cu-
rated synthetic corpus is a more effective and self-
consistent defence against catastrophic forget-
ting than external data mixing or layer freezing,
while still enhancing in-domain performance.
5
Analysis
5.1
Sensitivity to the SA-SFT Mix Ratio
To assess how sensitive SA-SFT is to the amount of
self-generated data and whether SA-SFT’s benefit
over UltraChat emerges only at a narrow span, we
carry out a mix-ratio sensitivity study.
Starting from the task-specific training set Dtask,
we create two augmented variants: (1) Task+UC:
Dtask mixed 1 : r with UltraChat data; (2) SA-SFT:
Dtask mixed 1 : r with self-generated data; where
the ratio r ∈{0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.25, 1.50
, 1.75}. For every setting we fine-tune LLaMA3-
8B-I. with LoRA under settings in Section 4, yield-
ing 14 checkpoints (7 ratios × 2 sources). We then
measure GSM8K and in-domain performance on
those models. The results are showed in Fig. 2.
Results.
Across both panels, we have the follow-
ing key findings:
1. SA-SFT
consistently
outperforms
the
Task+UC at every tested ratio, indicating
that its advantage is not confined to a narrow
sweet-spot.
2. Fig. 2a shows a general rise in GSM8K ac-
curacy for both mixing strategies as the ratio
r increases: adding more generic data can
better restore general capabilities.
3. Fig. 2b shows a sharp drop once r > 1.25.
Excessive generic data begins to dilute task-
specific signal, harming downstream tasks.
Balancing these observations, a 1 : 1 mix (r =
1.0) offers the best trade-off—maximizing general-
domain retention without eroding in-domain accu-
racy. Crucially, SA-SFT ’s self-generation feature
grants practitioners fine-grained control over the
data size, allowing them to tailor the mix to any
task without hunting for external corpus.
5.2
Step-Level Trajectory of Forgetting
To pinpoint when catastrophic forgetting emerges
and how different fine-tuning regimes alter its
course, we fine-tuned LLaMA3-8B-Instruct for


--- Page 7 ---
0
50
100
150
200
250
57.5
60.0
62.5
65.0
67.5
70.0
72.5
75.0
SA-SFT (Ours)
Task-Only
Task+UC
Task+GSM8K
Task+Math-Instruct
Figure 3: GSM8K accuracy over training steps.
three epochs and saved a snapshot every 40 optimi-
sation steps (0 →240). At each snapshot we evalu-
ated GSM8K accuracy. The experiment compared
five otherwise identical regimes that differ only in
the auxiliary data blended with the downstream task
corpus: (i)Task-only; (ii) Task+UC (1:1 mix); (iii)
SA-SFT-our method (1:1 mix); (iv) Task+Math-
Instruct (Yue et al., 2024) (1:1 mix of an external
mathematics corpus); (v) Task+GSM8K (approxi-
mately 1:0.7 mix of GSM8K’s own train split);
Fig. 3 yields the following insights: 1. The
SA-SFT curve remains essentially flat and consis-
tently achieves the highest accuracy, indicating that
self-generated data can provide stable and robust
protection for math reasoning throughout the en-
tire fine-tuning process. 2. The Task+UC curve
consistently under performs compared to SA-SFT
and exhibits greater fluctuation, suggesting less
stable resistance to forgetting. 3. Task-only ex-
hibits the canonical monotonic decline, confirm-
ing significant forgetting when fine-tuning on spe-
cific downstream tasks without any augmentation.
4. Task+GSM8K follows a U-shaped trajectory:
performance drops sharply at first and then grad-
ually recovers, indicating that mixing data from
even the same distribution as the evaluation set
does not prevent forgetting, but instead causes
the model to forget and then learn new knowl-
edge (or adapt to new distribution). This recov-
ery process is slow, in our experiment, even by
step 240—when the model has already fit the fine–
tuning task well—it still has not fully regained
its math ability. 5. Task+Math-Instruct improves
marginally over Task-Only, implying that sim-
ply injecting high-quality—but distribution-mis-
matched—math corpus cannot restore forgotten
skills within the 240-step budget; much of the com-
pute is likely spent adapting to new style, rather
than reinforcing transferable reasoning skills.
Model
MATH
LOGIC
CODE
OTHER
LLaMA3-8B-I.
45
17
38
1900
Qwen2.5-7B-I.
74
29
118
1779
Table 3: Density of math/logic/code content in 2 000
self-generated dialogues (absolute counts)
Implications.
The contrast between SA-SFT ’s
flat trajectory and the recovery-after-collapse seen
with Task+GSM8K highlights a crucial distinction:
preventing forgetting during training versus repair-
ing it afterwards.
Generating a model-specific
corpus before fine-tuning offers a lightweight,
computation-efficient safeguard that outperforms
both open-domain and in-domain external datasets.
5.3
Impact of Generator Model
One of our core claim: SA-SFT is most effective
when the synthetic corpus is produced by the very
model that will later be fine-tuned. A natural
follow-up question is: How sensitive is this benefit
to the choice of generator? If we depart from the
“same-model” principle—using a generator that
differs in architecture, training recipe, or parameter
size—does the resulting synthetic data still curb
forgetting, or does the mismatch degrade its value?
To answer this, we fix LLaMA3-8B-Instruct as
the base model and consider five alternative gen-
erators: (1) Qwen2.5-7B-I., (2) LLaMA2-7B-C.,
(3) LLaMA2-13B-C., (4) LLaMA3.2-3B-I., (5)
LLaMA3-70B-I.. For every generator we keep all
other hyper-parameters identical to the Section 4;
the synthetic corpus size is matched 1 : 1 to the fine-
tuning tasks. Each combined dataset is then fine-
tuned on the base model using exactly the same
LoRA configuration as before. Finally, we evalu-
ate: (i) in-domain performance on the task datasets
and (ii) general competence on GSM8K, MMLU,
IFEval, MedText, and AGIEval_G (5-shot, Med-
Text reported with ROUGE-L).
Table 2 corroborates our central claim. Across
the five general benchmarks, the strongest protec-
tion against forgetting (four of the five best scores)
arises when the synthetic corpus is produced by the
same LLaMA3-8B-Instruct model that is later fine-
tuned. The next-best result comes from data gen-
erated by the much larger LLaMA3-70B-Instruct,
yet still falls short of the self-generated variant.
On the in-domain task,
self-augmentation
again performs competitively (second only to the
LLaMA2-13B-C. generator), while the latter suf-


--- Page 8 ---
fers a severe drop on the general benchmarks—a
trade-off that self-generated data avoids. It is to be
noted that, on public leaderboards (Fourrier et al.,
2024) , Qwen2.5-7B-I. and LLaMA3-70B-I. both
outrank the 8B model. Their synthetic data indeed
sharpen instruction-following ability (see the IFE-
val column), but they do not replicate the unique
anti-forgetting effect of “same-model” data.
Taken together, these results indicate that higher-
quality but stylistically mismatched data cannot
match the effectiveness of truly self-generated data.
5.4
What Really Shields Models from
Forgetting?
The preceding experiments show that inserting a
short SA-SFT phase almost completely restores
GSM8K accuracy, but why does it work? Two
competing explanations exist:
A. The self-generated corpus happens to contain
critical facts and skills (e.g. mathematical
reasoning) that are later replayed during SFT,
directly inoculating the model.
B. The corpus merely matches the model’s
own latent distribution; any low–perplexity
text—regardless of topic—tempers the large,
style–driven parameter shifts responsible for
format-induced forgetting.
We distinguish A and B via a two-step study:
Step 1: How Much Math/Logic Is in the Data?
Protocol.
We select tow generators: LLaMA3-
8B-I. and Qwen2.5-7B-I.—we uniformly sample
2 000 dialogues. Each dialogue is classified by
GPT-4o, which assigns one of four mutually ex-
clusive labels: (i)MATH (arithmetic, algebra, word
problems); (ii) LOGIC (syllogisms, truth tables,
grid puzzles); (iii)CODE (programming, debugging,
algorithms); (iv)OTHER (all remaining content).
Findings.
Table 3 reveals that strictly math-
related material is scarce: only ≈5 % of the self-
generated dialogues touch mathematics, logic, or
code for LLaMA3, (≈11 % for Qwen2.5). Despite
this modest share, Section 4 showed a great recov-
ery on GSM8K, hinting that factors beyond mere
replay of math problems may be at work.
Step 2: Removing All Math/Logic/Code
Construction of “logic-free” corpora.
Using
the same GPT-4o filter, we delete every dialogue
Regime
LLaMA3-8B-I.
Qwen2.5-7B-I.
Base
75.8
82.0
Task-Only
65.6
77.7
SA-SFT FULL DATA
74.2
82.1
SA-SFT NOLOGIC
73.7
82.3
Table 4: GSM8K accuracy (%) after SFT with and
without math/logic/code content.
labelled MATH, LOGIC, or CODE from the self-
generated corpora of these two models. We then
down-sample the data to a fixed 10 000 pairs to
keep the ratio to 1:1, obtaining two new datasets:
LLaMA3-NoLogic and Qwen2.5-NoLogic.
Fine-tuning.
We repeat LoRA fine-tuning un-
der the identical settings of Section 4 and evaluate
on GSM8K on LLaMA3-8B-I. and Qwen2.5-7B-I.
models. Table 4 shows the outcome.
Key result: Even after purging every trace of
math/logic/code, SA-SFT data still prevents most
of the GSM8K accuracy drop.
We therefore support explanation B: we argue
that a substantial portion of catastrophic forget-
ting in SFT arises from style-induced gradient drift
rather than the loss of underlying knowledge. By
mixing self-generated samples that are pretraining-
aligned in surface form, SA-SFT counteracts such
spurious stylistic updates, thereby preserving gen-
eral capabilities while learning the target task. The-
oretical analysis is provided in Section A.
6
Conclusion
Catastrophic forgetting need not be the price of spe-
cialization. With a brief “self-talk” phase, our Self-
Augmented SFT (SA-SFT) shields LLMs from
skill loss while boosting task accuracy—without ex-
ternal data, extra losses, or manual curation. Across
five public benchmarks, SA-SFT not only recov-
ers general ability and strengthens in-domain per-
formance, but also consistently outperforms main-
stream baselines, offering the most stable balance
between retention and adaptation. Our theoreti-
cal analysis further explains this robustness: self-
generated, in-distribution text implicitly regularizes
training by counteracting style-induced parameter
drift. Because SA-SFT integrates seamlessly into
existing fine-tuning pipelines with minimal over-
head, it provides an immediate, low-effort safe-
guard for building stable and adaptive LLMs.


--- Page 9 ---
7
Limitations
While SA-SFT is simple and effective, it also has
several limitations.
Dependence on the base model’s generative qual-
ity.
SA-SFT relies on the frozen base model to
produce a self-authored corpus that is sufficiently
diverse and well-formed. For weaker or poorly
instruction-tuned models, self-dialogues may be-
come repetitive, low-information, or biased toward
shallow conversational patterns, which can reduce
the regularization effect and may even introduce
noise into training. In such cases, SA-SFT may re-
quire more careful prompt design or basic filtering
to avoid degenerate samples.
Incomplete coverage of all forgetting mech-
anisms.
Our analysis highlights style-induced
parameter drift as an important contributor to
catastrophic forgetting, and SA-SFT is particularly
suited to counteracting this failure mode via self-
aligned text. However, the true causes of catas-
trophic forgetting are still actively debated and
likely multifactorial: existing work attributes for-
getting to different sources, including gradient in-
terference and representation drift, task similarity
and sequential training effects, and the interaction
between optimization dynamics and parameter im-
portance (Kemker et al., 2018; Ramasesh et al.,
2021; Luo et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2025). As a
result, SA-SFT should be viewed as a targeted miti-
gation for the mechanism we identify and formal-
ize. While our empirical results suggest that self-
aligned rehearsal can be effective in many practical
settings, we do not claim it resolves all possible
causes of forgetting, nor do we deeply investigate
regimes where other mechanisms dominate.
Extra generation cost and potential data risks.
Although SA-SFT avoids external datasets and ad-
ditional loss terms, it still introduces an upfront
sampling step to create Dself, which incurs extra
inference-time compute and storage—especially
when scaling to many tasks or continual updates.
Moreover, self-generated data can contain inaccu-
racies, unsafe content, or spurious biases inherited
from the base model, which may be amplified when
mixed into fine-tuning. Practical deployments may
therefore benefit from inexpensive safeguards (e.g.,
deduplication, toxicity screening, or simple quality
heuristics) to reduce such risks without undermin-
ing the method’s lightweight nature.
References

arXiv preprint
arXiv:2311.07911.
A
Theoretical Analysis:
Style-Mismatch–Induced Forgetting
A.1
Intuitive Explanation
When fine-tuned on external SFT datasets, a pre-
trained language model often encounters stylis-
tic conventions—templated instruction formats,
repeated punctuation, or specific discourse pat-
terns—that rarely appear in its pretraining corpus.
These stylistic artifacts trigger large gradient up-
dates as the optimizer attempts to mimic the new
“surface form.” Such updates, however, carry little
semantic information and primarily distort param-
eter directions that were tuned to the pretraining
style. Consequently, the model experiences catas-
trophic forgetting: performance on general reason-
ing and pretraining-aligned tasks declines, not be-
cause the underlying knowledge is overwritten, but
because the parameters have been rotated toward
an unfamiliar stylistic regime.
Our framework SA-SFT proposes that self-
generated mixing alleviates this issue.
When
we interleave a portion of self-generated sam-
ples—whose linguistic style matches the model’s
own pretraining distribution—the optimizer’s up-
dates become more style-balanced. Gradients aris-
ing from familiar, in-distribution text statistically
cancel the superficial stylistic components intro-
duced by the external SFT corpus. As a result,
the parameter trajectory remains closer to the pre-
training manifold, preserving previously acquired
capabilities while still absorbing task-specific in-
formation.
A.2
Formal Setup
Let fω be a pretrained model with parameters ω0,
and let ℓ(·, ·) denote the per-token cross-entropy
loss. For a distribution D,
RD(ω) = E(x,y)∼D[ℓ(fω(x), y)].
(4)
Denote by D0 the pretraining distribution and by
Dtask the external SFT dataset. After T fine-tuning
steps from initialization ω0, the amount of forget-
ting is quantified as:
∆forget = RD0(ωT ) −RD0(ω0).
(5)
The expected gradient at initialization is:
gtask(ω0) = E(x,y)∼Dtask[∇ωℓ(fω0(x), y)].
(6)
We interpret each example as generated from latent
content c and style s, where s captures superficial
linguistic factors such as formatting or register.
A.3
Style–Semantic Decomposition
Empirically, gradients can be approximately de-
composed into two subspaces:
Assumption 1 (Approximate subspace sepa-
rability): There exists a Style subspace S ⊂Rd
and its orthogonal complement S⊥(the Semantic
subspace) such that
gtask ≈gsty + gsem,
gsty ∈S; gsem ∈S⊥.
(7)
Here gsty captures gradients that primarily change
surface form, while gsem modifies content-related
representations. When the stylistic distribution of
Dtask deviates strongly from the pretraining distri-
bution S0, the Style component gsty tends to domi-
nate, producing parameter drift unrelated to seman-
tic improvement.
To quantify this mismatch, we define the style
perplexity gap:
∆ppl = Ex∼Dtask[−log pω0(x)]
−Ex∼D0[−log pω0(x)].
(8)
A larger ∆ppl indicates that the SFT text is less
typical under the base model’s pretraining style,
correlating with larger ||gsty|| and stronger forget-
ting.


--- Page 12 ---
A.4
First-Order View of Forgetting
Assuming local L-smoothness of RD0 and an SGD
update rule ωt+1 = ωt −ηgtask(ωt), a first-order
Taylor expansion yields:
∆forget ≈−
*
∇RD0(ω0),
T
X
t=1
ηgtask(ωt−1)
+
+ O
␀L
X
t
η2||gtask(ωt−1)||2
.
(9)
This expression shows that forgetting arises
when the cumulative update has a large projection
onto directions that increase pretraining loss. The
complete derivation of this result is provided in
Section B.2. If the Style component dominates
the update trajectory, the inner product above be-
comes positive, leading to observable degradation
on pretraining-aligned benchmarks even in the ab-
sence of semantic conflict.
A.5
Effect of SA-SFT
Let Dself be the distribution of model-generated
samples. For a mixing ratio ε = λ/(1+λ) induced
by dataset concatenation,
Dmix = (1 −ε)Dtask + εDself,
gmix = (1 −ε)gtask + εgself.
(10)
Because self-generated text is style-aligned with
the pretraining distribution, we have gself ≈g0, and
thus
ProjS(gmix) ≈(1 −ε)ProjS(gtask),
(11)
implying that self-mixing linearly attenuates the
Style component of the gradient. The complete
derivation is provided in Section B.4.
Under
the same local-smoothness assumption, the cor-
responding forgetting can be upper-bounded (infor-
mally) as:
∆(mix)
forget ≲(1 −ε)∆(task)
forget + O(L
X
t
η2||gmix||2),
(12)
suggesting that moderate ratios (e.g., ε ≈0.5)
optimally suppress style-induced drift while main-
taining sufficient task signal. Notably, replacing
Dself with data generated by other models is less
effective: their stylistic distribution Sother deviates
from S0, resulting in weaker cancellation of style
gradients despite potential semantic richness.
B
Derivations and Additional Details
B.1
Preliminaries and Notation
Let R(ω) ≡RD0(ω) denote the pretraining risk,
and let {ωt}T
t=0 be the parameter iterates under
SGD on the SFT distribution Dtask with step size
η > 0:
ωt+1 = ωt −η gtask(ωt),
gtask(ωt) = ∇ω E(x,y)∼Dtask

ℓ(fω(x), y)

. (13)
We write ∆ωt := ωt+1 −ωt = −η gtask(ωt) and
∆forget := R(ωT ) −R(ω0).
B.2
Local Smoothness and Two-Sided
First-Order Remainder
We assume local L-smoothness of R around ω0:
for all ω1, ω2 in a neighborhood of ω0,
∥∇R(ω1) −∇R(ω2)∥≤L ∥ω1 −ω2∥.
(14)
Equivalently, the Taylor remainder is two-sided
bounded:
 R(ω2) −R(ω1) −⟨∇R(ω1), ω2 −ω1⟩

≤
L
2 ∥ω2 −ω1∥2.
(15)
Applying (15) with ω1 = ωt and ω2 = ωt+1 = ωt+
∆ωt yields the per-step identity with a bounded
remainder:
R(ωt+1) −R(ωt) = ⟨∇R(ωt), ∆ωt⟩
|
{z
}
first-order term
+ εt,
|εt| ≤
L
2 ∥∆ωt∥2.
(16)
Summing (16) over t = 0, . . . , T −1 gives
∆forget =
T−1
X
t=0
⟨∇R(ωt), ∆ωt⟩+
T−1
X
t=0
εt,

T−1
X
t=0
εt
 ≤
L
2
T−1
X
t=0
∥∆ωt∥2.
(17)
Using ∆ωt = −η gtask(ωt),
∆forget = −η
T−1
X
t=0

∇R(ωt), gtask(ωt)

+ E1,
|E1| ≤
Lη2
2
T−1
X
t=0
∥gtask(ωt)∥2.
(18)


--- Page 13 ---
B.3
Linearization at ω0 and the First-Order
Expansion
To expose the directional alignment driving forget-
ting, we linearize ∇R(ωt) at ω0:
∇R(ωt) = ∇R(ω0) + δt,
∥δt∥≤L ∥ωt −ω0∥.
(19)
Since ωt−ω0 = Pt−1
j=0 ∆ωj = −η Pt−1
j=0 gtask(ωj),
we obtain the bound
∥δt∥≤L η
t−1
X
j=0
∥gtask(ωj)∥.
(20)
Plugging ∇R(ωt) = ∇R(ω0)+δt into (18) yields
∆forget = −η

∇R(ω0),
T−1
X
t=0
gtask(ωt)

−η
T−1
X
t=0
⟨δt, gtask(ωt)⟩+ E1.
(21)
The drift term is bounded by Cauchy–Schwarz
and (20):
η
T−1
X
t=0
⟨δt, gtask(ωt)⟩
 ≤η
T−1
X
t=0
∥δt∥∥gtask(ωt)∥
≤L η2
T−1
X
t=0
t−1
X
j=0
∥gtask(ωj)∥∥gtask(ωt)∥.
(22)
Under small steps and bounded gradients (the
usual local analysis regime), the double sum is
O
␀ P
t ∥gtask(ωt)∥2
. Hence we arrive at the signed
first-order expansion with a two-sided remainder:
∆forget = −

∇R(ω0),
T
X
t=1
η gtask(ωt−1)

+ E,
|E| ≤C L
T
X
t=1
η2 ∥gtask(ωt−1)∥2,
(23)
for some absolute constant C > 0. Equation (23)
justifies the main-text use of “≈” together with an
O(·) remainder: it is an equality up to a bounded,
second-order term whose sign is a priori unknown.
B.4
Effect of Mixing Self-Generated Data
Let Dself be a self-generated distribution (by fω0),
and define the mixture
Dmix = (1 −ε) Dtask + ε Dself,
gmix(ω) = (1 −ε) gtask(ω) + ε gself(ω).
(24)
Assumption (Style–Semantic subspace).
There
exists a subspace S (Style) and its orthogonal com-
plement S⊥(Semantic) such that gtask = gsty+gsem
with gsty ∈S, gsem ∈S⊥.
Moreover, self-
generated samples are style-aligned with D0, so
that ProjS gself(ω0) ≈0. Then, at initialization,
ProjS gmix(ω0) = (1 −ε) ProjS gtask(ω0)
+ ε ProjS gself(ω0) ≈(1 −ε) ProjS gtask(ω0).
(25)
Replacing gtask with gmix in (23) yields
∆(mix)
forget =

∇R(ω0), PT
t=1 η gmix(ωt−1)

+ E(mix).
(26)
Combining (25) with the subspace assumption
shows that the Style component of the signed main
term is reduced by a factor (1 −ε), while the Se-
mantic component is preserved. Under the same
local-smoothness and small-step conditions,
E(mix) ≤C L
T
X
t=1
η2 ∥gmix(ωt−1)∥2,
(27)
and the Style-driven part of ∆(mix)
forget scales roughly
as (1 −ε) times that of ∆(task)
forget, formalizing the
attenuation effect reported in the main text.
B.5
Remarks on the Style Perplexity Gap
The quantity
∆ppl = Ex∼Dtask

−log pω0(x)

−Ex∼D0

−log pω0(x)

(28)
is a proxy for stylistic out-of-distribution dis-
tance measured by fω0.
Since −log pω0(x) =
P
t −log pω0(xt | x<t), it is the standard sequence
NLL under the base model. Empirically, larger
∆ppl correlates with larger ∥ProjS gtask(ω0)∥, con-
sistent with the view that style mismatch enlarges
the Style component of the update.
